foundation.title: STRUCTURE
foundation.subtitle: Structure of a Program
foundation.content: A C program structure starts with imports at the top and fundamentally MUST have an 'int main()' as the entry point. Statements are lines ending sequentially in semicolons, and the strict order of execution flows intensely top-to-bottom.
foundation.scenario: A Formal Letter. You aggressively must start with the correct Header Room (#include), the correct Greeting (main function), and you MUST cleanly end every single logical action with a period (Semicolon).
foundation.learningOutcome: Code runs line by line in order

examples.title: Top-to-Bottom
examples.explanation: Lines execute vertically. If 'moveUp' is before 'moveDown', player goes UP first.

foundation.keyPoint: Programs run top-to-bottom natively starting from main().

foundation.title: PRINT
foundation.subtitle: Print Statements
foundation.content: C strictly uses 'printf()' to blast output display data to the terminal. It prints standard raw text string sequences and fundamentally separates visual text from calculating pure numbers.
foundation.scenario: A Mechanical Typewriter. When you hit a key, it prints exactly a letter linearly. But if you want to start a new line massively below, you manually crank the return lever.

foundation.title: FORMAT
foundation.subtitle: Output Formatting
foundation.content: C inherently demands you to manually combine formatting text heavily using specific '% ' markers. You insert precise spaces, new line symbols (\n), and seamlessly blend raw multiple values natively.
foundation.scenario: A Fill-in-the-Blank Letter. 'Dear %s, your balance is %d'. The %s and %d are natively empty slots where you insert the real Name and dynamic Math sequentially at the end.

foundation.title: VARIABLE
foundation.subtitle: Variables (What/Why)
foundation.content: Variables natively act as absolute containers radically storing dynamic basic data inside raw memory (RAM). You severely securely need them primarily because raw program values actively literally change.
foundation.scenario: A physical RAM memory Moving Box. You strictly logically label it 'Score' (Name), and lock an integer inside natively (Value). Tomorrow, the score might increase massively.

foundation.title: NAME_RULES
foundation.subtitle: Naming Rules
foundation.content: Variables mandate strictly rigid naming rules natively. Standard Meaningful naming explicitly uses characters a-z/A-Z/0-9/_. Case sensitivity universally exists, spaces are absolutely forbidden, and leading massive numbers shatter logic.
foundation.scenario: A Linux Terminal Password. You essentially cannot use wild invalid emojis or vast empty spaces. The strict parser literally radically demands an array of exact standard sequential characters.
foundation.learningOutcome: Write valid and readable variable names

foundation.title: DATATYPES
foundation.subtitle: Data Types
foundation.content: The C language strictly explicitly relies fundamentally on massive discrete hardware primitive Data Types heavily designed for native speed: basic Integer, standard Float, singular Array Strings, and rudimentary pseudo Boolean logic ideas.
foundation.scenario: A Specialty Shipping Container. A delicate 'Milk' container cannot massively transport aggressive 'Gasoline'. You fundamentally absolutely must explicitly accurately type the container natively so the C OS allocates precise safe RAM.

foundation.title: COMMENTS
foundation.subtitle: Ignored Text
foundation.content: Comments are single-line readable English notes specifically for programmers. The entire rigid C Compiler brutally ignores them conceptually.
foundation.scenario: A Sticky Note on an Engine Valve. The note vividly screams 'Do not touch the red wire!'. The core engine entirely ignores the paper note, but the human perfectly observes it.

foundation.title: KEYWORDS
foundation.subtitle: Keywords
foundation.content: Keywords are conceptually reserved protected words that C utilizes for pure internal architecture. You absolutely cannot ever hijack them as variable names.
foundation.scenario: Reserved VIP Parking. The critical spot legally marked 'CEO' (Keyword syntax) cannot be casually seized by 'Employee Bob' (Variable). The complex system will heavily aggressively reject you.

foundation.title: CASE
foundation.subtitle: Case Sensitivity
foundation.content: C exhibits immense case sensitivity. Uppercase mismatch triggers intense errors instantly. A single swapped letter literally creates completely disconnected names in the massive dictionary.
foundation.scenario: Two Cloned Twins. They appear physically visually identical, but one is tagged 'John' and the other specifically 'john'. In C's relentless eyes, they are legally two strictly disconnected objects in RAM.
foundation.learningOutcome: Avoid logical errors due to case mismatch

foundation.keyPoint: Case determines strict memory maps flawlessly. 'X' is heavily different than 'x'.
